coredata.dc:title: Fullerol–graphene nanobuds: Novel water dispersible and highly conductive nanocarbon for electrochemical sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Bourlinos, Athanasios B.
Georgakilas, Vasilios
Mouselimis, Vasilios
Kouloumpis, Antonios
Mouzourakis, Eleftherios
Koutsioukis, Apostolos
Antoniou, Myrsini-Kyriaki
Gournis, Dimitrios
Karakassides, Michael A.
Deligiannakis, Yiannis
Urbanova, Veronika
Cepe, Klara
Bakandritsos, Aristides
Zboril, Radek
coredata.dc:description: The covalent functionalization of graphene enabling to overcome its hydrophobicity and still keeping a high conductivity is a challenging task, particularly for advanced biosensing applications. We present a facile strategy towards water dispersible nanocarbon with excellent conductive and sensing properties. Graphene–fullerol (GFL) covalent hybrid is prepared by impregnation of chemically exfoliated graphene sheets with fullerol clusters followed by thermal treatment. The partial dehydroxylation of fullerol during heating is necessary for exposing the fullerene skeleton to graphene and promoting their covalent bonding. The remaining hydroxyl and ionic groups equip the final product with good dispersibility in water (0.04mgmL−1). Simultaneously, the GFL hybrid retains a high electrical conductivity of 0.8×105 Sm−1, which is comparable to that of unmodified graphene. The conductive and hydroxyl-bearing derivative was successfully applied for electrochemical sensing of dopamine, hydroquinone (HQ), and catechol (C) using cyclic voltammetry. GFL modified electrodes exhibited simultaneous HQ and C detection at lower potentials compared to other nanosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene nanobud
Fullerol
Hybrid conductive
Waterborne
Electrochemical sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and tentative structure of the water-dispersible graphene–fullerol (GFL) hybrid.

(A) FT-IR and (B) Raman spectra of G380, GFL and FL380. AFM images of (C) G380 and (D) GFL. (E) HR-TEM image of GFL. (F) Dark field HR-TEM image of a GFL flake which was analyzed with EDS chemical mapping. (G–I) Carbon, oxygen, and sodium chemical maps of a GFL flake.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms recorded with GCE modified with GFL, G and unmodified GCE at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. (B) Electrochemical impedance spectra recorded with GCE modified with GFL, G, and unmodified GCE at an applied frequency range from 0.1 to 100kHz with AC amplitude of 5mV. All the measurements were performed in 0.1M KCl containing 5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox probe. (C) Differential pulse voltammograms (DPV) of a mixture containing 100μM catechol (C) and 100μM hydroquinone (HQ) in phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) recorded with unmodified GCE, modified with G and GFL. (D) Cyclic voltammograms of 1mM dopamine in phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0) recorded at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 with unmodified GCE, modified with G and GFL.

head.ce:title: Fullerol–graphene nanobuds: Novel water dispersible and highly conductive nanocarbon for electrochemical sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, University of Patras, 26504 Rio Patras, Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, University of Ioannina

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Regional Centre of Advanced Technologies and Materials, Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Palacký University in Olomouc, Šlechtitelů 27, 783 71 Olomouc, Czech Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Regional Centre of Advanced Technologies and Materials, Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Palacký University in Olomouc

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The covalent functionalization of graphene enabling to overcome its hydrophobicity and still keeping a high conductivity is a challenging task, particularly for advanced biosensing applications. We present a facile strategy towards water dispersible nanocarbon with excellent conductive and sensing properties. Graphene–fullerol (GFL) covalent hybrid is prepared by impregnation of chemically exfoliated graphene sheets with fullerol clusters followed by thermal treatment. The partial dehydroxylation of fullerol during heating is necessary for exposing the fullerene skeleton to graphene and promoting their covalent bonding. The remaining hydroxyl and ionic groups equip the final product with good dispersibility in water (0.04mgmL−1). Simultaneously, the GFL hybrid retains a high electrical conductivity of 0.8×105 Sm−1, which is comparable to that of unmodified graphene. The conductive and hydroxyl-bearing derivative was successfully applied for electrochemical sensing of dopamine, hydroquinone (HQ), and catechol (C) using cyclic voltammetry. GFL modified electrodes exhibited simultaneous HQ and C detection at lower potentials compared to other nanosensors.

ce:sections.ce:para: Carbon plays a crucial role in materials science while merging of different allotropes can create all-carbon superstructures with useful properties for various applications [1–4]. Particularly, the design of hybrids based on graphene and fullerene attracts interest due to the unique electronic properties of graphene and the exotic chemistry of fullerene. Such attributes may give access to new graphene derivatives with well-defined structure and electronic properties [5–7].

Moreover, in the field of graphene chemistry, one of the goals is to explore a suitable functionalization enabling to overcome the graphene hydrophobicity and, at the same time, to keep/improve its high conductivity. Graphene nanobuds represent a new class of hybrids offering such properties through the attachment of fullerenes onto graphene by covalent bonding [7] and can be regarded as the 2D analogue of nanobuds based on carbon nanotubes [8]. Graphene nanobuds provide the possibility to selectively functionalize the embedded C60 molecules without further compromising the sp 2 structure of the less reactive graphene network [7,9]. This allows to synthesize soluble derivatives with excellent conductivity [7].

Fullerol is an important polyhydroxy-fullerene which, due to its phenolic acidity, can be neutralized with NaOH to afford water-soluble ionic clusters (–O−Na+, sodium salt) [10,11]. Ionic fullerols have been already exploited in the covalent modification of carbon nanotubes towards aqueous-dispersible hybrids [12] as well as in the modification of graphene oxide through reaction with the oxygen-containing functionalities of the substrate [13,14]. Nevertheless, in the literature, there is no report on fullerol–graphene covalent hybrids prepared through direct bonding of fullerol on high quality graphene. High quality graphene is largely preferred on account of its well-defined structure and excellent electronic properties. It is typically obtained from graphite by the liquid-phase exfoliation technique [15,16]. Fullerol is expected to introduce exchange sites/functional groups (–O−Na+) and hydrophilicity to graphene, which lacks these properties for certain important applications, such as electrochemical sensing. In addition, the combination of sp 2 carbon allotropes is expected to afford conductive hybrids where, in terms of electronic properties, the attachment of sp 2 carbon clusters on graphene may compensate any disruption of its basal plane through extensive conjugation of the bonded counterparts.

Graphene was obtained by the liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite in DMF followed by evaporation of the solvent. The fullerol used in this work has the general formula of C120O30(OH)30·30H2O25Na+. Initially, the ingredients are mixed in DMF/water, where fullerol is easily washed away by water. Aqueous leaching is still observable even after heating at 350°C. Importantly, covalent hybrids can be only obtained at 380–400°C, where fullerol does not wash off with water (Fig. S1 in Supplementary material). Temperatures higher than 400°C might be detrimental for the fullerol clusters. The solubility and colloidal stability of the hybrid drop as moving from 380 to 400°C, however, conductivity rather seems to increase. GFL exhibits an ionic character, evidenced by its highly negative zeta-potential (ζ =−40mV in H2O), which is ascribed to the attachment of the fullerol groups. Experiments showed that fullerol itself keeps its ionic character up to 400°C, being highly soluble in water and having the same ζ of −30mV as the starting fullerol. On the contrary, pristine graphene becomes completely insoluble after heat-treatment at 380°C (sample termed as G380). In spite of the large negative ζ value, GFL settles down within a few days but fully re-disperses in water by mild shaking. It also displays the typical precipitation reactions of fullerol with Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, and H+ ions [18]. However, unlike the fullerol salt, the hybrid does not give a precipitate with cetyltrimethylammonium ions. This could be explained by strong interactions between the hydrophobic tail of the surfactant and graphene towards the formation of charge-stabilized aqueous colloids [19].

Graphene was obtained by the liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite in DMF followed by evaporation of the solvent. The fullerol used in this work has the general formula of C120O30(OH)30·30H2O25Na+. Initially, the ingredients are mixed in DMF/water, where fullerol is easily washed away by water. Aqueous leaching is still observable even after heating at 350°C. Importantly, covalent hybrids can be only obtained at 380–400°C, where fullerol does not wash off with water (Fig. S1 in Supplementary material). Temperatures higher than 400°C might be detrimental for the fullerol clusters. The solubility and colloidal stability of the hybrid drop as moving from 380 to 400°C, however, conductivity rather seems to increase. GFL exhibits an ionic character, evidenced by its highly negative zeta-potential (ζ =−40mV in H2O), which is ascribed to the attachment of the fullerol groups. Experiments showed that that fullerol itself keeps its ionic character up to 400°C, being highly soluble in water and having the same ζ of −30mV as the starting fullerol. On the contrary, pristine graphene becomes completely insoluble after heat-treatment at 380°C (sample termed as G380). In spite of the large negative ζ value, GFL settles down within a few days but fully re-disperses in water by mild shaking. It also displays the typical precipitation reactions of fullerol with Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, and H+ ions [18]. However, unlike the fullerol salt, the hybrid does not give a precipitate with cetyltrimethylammonium ions. This could be explained by strong interactions between the hydrophobic tail of the surfactant and graphene towards the formation of charge-stabilized aqueous colloids [19].

Further insights in the processes taking place during heat-treatment of the individual components were provided by termogravimetric analysis/differential thermal analysis (TGA/DTA) under air (Fig. S2 in Supplementary material). Graphene combustion occurs at around 600°C, evidencing its high quality [20]. Amorphous carbon or heavily oxidized graphene combusts between 300–400°C. On the other hand, fullerol shows the first weight loss below 200°C due to the release of crystal water molecules (ca. 15% w/w) and another one between 200 and 400°C due to dehydroxylation (ca. 15%, w/w) [21]. These weight losses agree well with the given formula of C120O30(OH)30·30H2O·25Na+. We speculate that the dehydroxylation step is important in order to expose the fullerene skeleton for further reaction with graphene. Thus, the synthesis of covalent hybrid takes place close to 400°C, i.e., a temperature at which no aqueous leaching of the fullerol is observed from the hybrid. Theoretical studies have been previously proposed explaining the bonding in carbon nanobuds and graphene-C60 hybrids [8,22–27]. On the contrary, the –O−Na+ groups are thermally more stable, thus imparting aqueous dispersibility and highly negative ζ to the product.

Further insights in the processes taking place during heat-treatment of the individual components were provided by termogravimetric analysis/differential thermal analysis (TGA/DTA) technique under air (Fig. S2 in Supplementary material). Graphene combustion occurs at around 600°C, evidencing its high quality [20]. Amorphous carbon or heavily oxidized graphene combusts between 300–400°C. On the other hand, fullerol shows the first weight loss below 200°C due to the release of crystal water molecules (ca. 15% w/w) and another one between 200 and 400°C due to dehydroxylation (ca. 15%, w/w) [21]. These weight losses agree well with the given formula of C120O30(OH)30·30H2O·25Na+. We speculate that the dehydroxylation step is important in order to expose the fullerene skeleton for further reaction with graphene. Thus, the synthesis of covalent hybrid takes place close to 400°C, i.e., a temperature at which no aqueous leaching of the fullerol is observed from the hybrid. Theoretical studies have been previously proposed explaining the bonding in carbon nanobuds and graphene-C60 hybrids [8,22–27]. On the contrary, the –O−Na+ groups are thermally more stable, thus imparting aqueous dispersibility and highly negative ζ to the product.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR; Fig. 2A) and UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S3 in Supplementary material) of the hybrid were compared to those of the FL380 (fullerol heated at 380°C) and G380 samples, to understand better the chemical structure of the product. G380 is IR-inactive as expected for high quality graphene. However, the FT-IR spectrum of GFL differentiates from that of FL380 in the CC fingerprint of fullerol around 1600cm−1 [28]. This band appears broad for FL380 but clearly resolves in two sharper peaks for GFL, due to the dispersed state of the fullerol clusters on graphene [29]. The fullerol-related bands at 1100cm−1 (v C–O) and 1440cm−1 (δ s C–O–H) remain unchanged in the hybrid. Turning to the optical spectra of the samples, GFL exhibits two broad shoulders also present in FL380 due to fullerol-related CC enol groups [21]. The low-intensity absorption at a higher wavelength corresponds to n–π* transition, whereas the high-intensity band at a lower wavelength corresponds to π–π* transition.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR; Fig. 2A) and UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S3 in Supplementary material) of the hybrid were compared to those of the FL380 (fullerol heated at 380°C) and G380 samples, to understand better the chemical structure of the product. G380 is IR-inactive as expected for high quality graphene. However, the FT-IR spectrum of GFL differentiates from that of FL380 in the CC fingerprint of fullerol around 1600cm−1 [28]. This band appears broad for FL380 but clearly resolves in two sharper peaks for GFL, due to the dispersed state of the fullerol clusters on graphene [29]. The fullerol-related bands at 1100cm−1 (v C–O) and 1440cm−1 (δ s C–O–H) remain unchanged in the hybrid. Turning to the optical spectra of the samples, GFL exhibits two broad shoulders also present in FL380 due to fullerol-related CC enol groups [21]. The low-intensity absorption at a higher wavelength corresponds to n–π* transition, whereas the high-intensity band at a lower wavelength corresponds to π–π* transition.

Further Raman spectroscopic studies on GFL and G380 showed the typical G (1570cm−1) and D (1348cm−1) bands of graphene-based materials (Fig. 2B). In both cases, the G band appears sharp (20–25cm−1 FWHM) and of high intensity, implying high quality graphene. The I D/I G ratio for GFL is 0.2 while that for G380 is 0.3. Hence, fullerol bonding to graphene decreases the defects of the carbon lattice. This finding agrees well with the healing effect previously reported for graphene nanobuds based on C60 [7]. In general, covalent modification of graphene is expected to cause an increase in the I D/I G ratio. Nevertheless, direct attachment of fullerene cages on graphene may eventually extend the sp 2 network, thus causing a drop in the I D/I G [29]. Bonding at the more reactive defect sites of graphene could further explain this trend. Finally, the Raman signal of FL380 is not contributing much to the Raman spectrum of GFL due to both the large FWHM and much larger number of graphitic carbons with respect to fullerol carbons in the hybrid. Evidence of the fullerols’ bonding on graphene was collected with atomic force microscopy (AFM). The G380 (Fig. 2C) and G samples (Fig. S4A in Supplementary material) are composed of flat flakes, unlike GFL (Fig. 2D), where the graphene flakes are densely decorated with relatively uniform and nearly spherical particles. In all cases, the flakes were identified as a single layer graphene (Fig. S4B and C in Supplementary material). The presence of single fullerol clusters on graphene was also confirmed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; Fig. 2E and Fig. S5 in Supplementary material), evidenced by the circular spots detected over the graphene flakes. An indication of bonding comes from the lack of roundness noticed for some of the attached clusters by the side facing the graphene surface (marked inside red circles, Fig. S5). In several cases the clusters seem fused to graphene near the interface (a tentative scheme is provided in Fig. S5, right). Similar fusing has been also observed for fullerene peapods inside CNTs, as well as, in the case of carbon nanobuds [30,31]. Chemical mapping of GFL with energy dispersive spectroscopy over a large area shows a homogeneous distribution of O and Na, stemming from fullerol –O−Na+ groups, over a dense carbon matrix from graphene (Fig. 2F–I), suggesting a similarly homogeneous functionalization of graphene with fullerols. The presence of fullerols is additionally verified by XPS, due to the significant presence of oxygen in both C–OH (hydroxyl) and O–C–OH (hemiketal) states, as well as due to the detection of 2at.% Na, attached as counterion on deprotonated acidic –C–OH groups (Fig. S6 and references therein).

Further Raman spectroscopic studies on GFL and G380 showed the typical G (1570cm−1) and D (1348cm−1) bands of graphene-based materials (Fig. 2B). In both cases, the G band appears sharp (20–25cm−1 FWHM) and of high intensity, implying high quality graphene. The I D/I G ratio for GFL is 0.2 while that for G380 is 0.3. Hence, fullerol bonding to graphene decreases the defects of the carbon lattice. This finding agrees well with the healing effect previously reported for graphene nanobuds based on C60 [7]. In general, covalent modification of graphene is expected to cause an increase in the I D/I G ratio. Nevertheless, direct attachment of fullerene cages on graphene may eventually extend the sp 2 network, thus causing a drop in the I D/I G [29]. Bonding at the more reactive defect sites of graphene could further explain this trend. Finally, the Raman signal of FL380 is not contributing much to the Raman spectrum of GFL due to both the large FWHM and much larger number of graphitic carbons with respect to fullerol carbons in the hybrid. Evidence of the fullerols’ bonding on graphene was collected with atomic force microscopy (AFM). The G380 (Fig. 2C) and G samples (Fig. S4A in Supplementary material) are composed of flat flakes, unlike GFL (Fig. 2D), where the graphene flakes are densely decorated with relatively uniform and nearly spherical particles. In all cases, the flakes were identified as a single layer graphene (Fig. S4B and C in Supplementary material). The presence of single fullerol clusters on graphene was also confirmed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; Fig. 2E and Fig. S5 in Supplementary material), evidenced by the circular spots detected over the graphene flakes. An indication of bonding comes from the lack of roundness noticed for some of the attached clusters by the side facing the graphene surface (marked inside red circles, Fig. S5). In several cases the clusters seem fused to graphene near the interface (a tentative scheme is provided in Fig. S5, right). Similar fusing has been also observed for fullerene peapods inside CNTs, as well as, in the case of carbon nanobuds [30,31]. Chemical mapping of GFL with energy dispersive spectroscopy over a large area shows a homogeneous distribution of O and Na, stemming from fullerol –O−Na+ groups, over a dense carbon matrix from graphene (Fig. 2F–I), suggesting a similarly homogeneous functionalization of graphene with fullerols. The presence of fullerols is additionally verified by XPS, due to the significant presence of oxygen in both C–OH (hydroxyl) and O–C–OH (hemiketal) states, as well as due to the detection of 2at.% Na, attached as counterion on deprotonated acidic –C–OH groups (Fig. S6 and references therein).

Owing to its hydrophilic character, GFL hybrid was deposited homogenously on common paper as circular spots (thickness of 4μm as evidenced by scanning electron microscopy SEM) that showed very low sheet resistance of 3Ωsq−1, which is translated to a remarkable conductivity of 0.8×105 Sm−1. This value is comparable to the electrical conductivity of high quality graphene-based materials [19,32–34]. Specifically, it is similar to that of CVD or spray-coated graphene (0.7×105 −1.3×105 Sm−1) reported by Ruoff and Geim-Novoselov groups (see Table 1 in reference 32) and close to the theoretical conductance quantum (∼105 Sm−1). On the other hand, this value is higher than those reported for reduced graphene oxide, typically in the range 102–103 Sm−1 [35]. In contrast, G380 showed much lower conductivity (∼10Sm−1) as a result of inhomogeneous deposition due to a poor dispersibility in water. In addition, G380 has more defect sites than GFL as evidenced by Raman spectroscopy, thus further deteriorating the electronic properties. On the other side, pristine graphene G with similar band positions and half-width (1570cm−1, 20cm−1) but lower I D/I G ratio (∼0.2) shows a higher conductivity (555Sm−1) compared to G380. Although Raman traits of pristine graphene are similar to those in the hybrid, GFL still exhibits a higher conductivity than G sample because of its much better dispersibility and homogeneous deposition.

The excellent dispersibility, the presence of –O− groups and the electrical properties of GFL motivated us to study the hybrid as an electrochemical sensor [36,37]. The electrochemical properties were first evaluated with modified glassy carbon electrodes by cyclic voltammetry in the presence of redox probe [Fe(CN)]4−/3− as shown in Fig. 3A. Heterogeneous electron transfer (HET), represented by peak-to-peak separation ΔE p, indicated faster electron transfer for the GFL modified electrode (ΔE p =127mV) as compared to the G modified electrode (ΔE p =207mV) or the bare electrode (ΔE p =144mV). The electron transfer properties were further characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in order to compare charge transfer resistance (R ct) that is determined by the diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot (Fig. 3B). The values of R ct were in good agreement with the results obtained by cyclic voltammetry since the lowest impedance was measured for the GFL modified electrode (163.7Ω), followed by the unmodified one (683.06Ω), while the G modified electrode displayed the highest R ct (1261.3Ω).

The superior electrochemical response of GFL was further explored for sensing hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (C), and dopamine. However, the direct and simultaneous determination of HQ and C at conventional electrodes such as a glassy carbon (GCE) is difficult because similar chemical structures lead to overlap of their redox peaks [38]. Fig. 3C shows that GFL demonstrated well-defined and resolved reduction peaks of HQ (0.45mV) and C (134.7mV) in their mixtures. It can be also seen that the sensitivity of the GFL modified electrode is much higher over the unmodified or G modified electrodes. If compared to other existing systems for simultaneous HQ and C detection, such as graphene/lanthanum hydroxide nanowires [39] or gold nanoparticles@carbon nanocages [40] composites, GFL exhibits higher conductivity and detection of HQ and C at lower potentials.

To further underpin the useful sensing properties of GFL, dopamine was chosen as an important biomolecule that is involved in many biological processes in the human brain and body. In this case, GFL modified electrode showed considerable improvement in terms of both overpotential reduction and sensitivity over unmodified or G modified electrode (Fig. 3D). Moreover, GFL showed better overall performance, i.e., lower potential of detection and better reversibility of the system compared to other biosensors based on reduced graphene oxide/carbon dots composites [41], PMo11V@N doped few layer graphene [42] or fullerene-hollow microsphere modified gold electrodes [43]. Overall, the results demonstrate that GFL is endowed with attractive electrochemical properties and thus provides the basis for further optimization as a promising sensing platform. Other potential target molecules may include nitro compounds, such as 2,4-dinitrotoluene (Fig. S7 in Supplementary material).

To further underpin the useful sensing properties of GFL, dopamine was chosen as an important biomolecule that is involved in many biological processes in the human brain and body. In this case, GFL modified electrode showed considerable improvement in terms of both over-potential reduction and sensitivity over unmodified or G modified electrode (Fig. 3D). Moreover, GFL showed better overall performance, i.e., lower potential of detection and better reversibility of the system compared to other biosensors based on reduced graphene oxide/carbon dots composites [41], PMo11V@N doped few layer graphene [42] or fullerene-hollow microsphere modified gold electrodes [43]. Overall, the results demonstrate that GFL is endowed with attractive electrochemical properties and thus provides the basis for further optimization as a promising sensing platform. Other potential target molecules may include nitro compounds, such as 2,4-dinitrotoluene (Fig. S7 in Supplementary material).

In summary, we have demonstrated the possibility of the covalent attachment of fullerol onto graphene through dehydroxylation of the cluster and subsequent bonding to graphene after heat treatment at 380°C. The resulting covalent hybrid exhibits aqueous dispersibility (0.04mgmL−1) and high electrical conductivity of 0.8×105 Sm−1. Such waterborne conductive graphene nanobuds could be used as conductive inks, fillers, thin films or sensors. In the latter case, we demonstrated that the fullerol–graphene nanobud is sensitive towards dopamine electrochemical detection with better sensing properties compared to graphene or its derivatives, while it can detect and discriminate between hydroquinone and catechol in mixtures thereof.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge support from the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic (LO1305 and CZ.1.05/2.1.00/19.0377) and the assistance (under Project No. LM2015073) provided by the Research Infrastructure NanoEnviCz, supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apmt.2017.05.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalization of graphene: covalent and non-covalent approaches, derivatives and applications
Broad family of carbon nanoallotropes: classification, chemistry, and applications of fullerenes, carbon dots, nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, and combined superstructures
Hydrophilic nanotube supported graphene–water dispersible carbon superstructure with excellent conductivity
Noncovalent functionalization of graphene and graphene oxide for energy materials, biosensing, catalytic, and biomedical applications
A novel route towards high quality fullerene-pillared graphene
A bottom-up approach for the synthesis of highly ordered fullerene-intercalated graphene hybrids
Graphene nanobuds: synthesis and selective organic derivatisation
Selective covalent functionalization of carbon nanobuds
Spectroscopic and photophysical properties of a highly derivatized C60 fullerol
Synthesis, characterization and nonlinear optical property of graphene-C60hybrid
Stable graphite exfoliation by fullerenol intercalation via aqueous route
Liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite towards solubilized graphenes
Aqueous-phase exfoliation of graphite in the presence of polyvinylpyrrolidone for the production of water-soluble graphenes
Highly conductive and dispersible graphene and its application in P3HT-based solar cells
Reaction of hydroxyfullerene with metal salts: a route to remediation and immobilization
Liquid phase production of graphene by exfoliation of graphite in surfactant/water solutions
High yield production and purification of few layer graphene by Gum Arabic assisted physical sonication
One-pot synthesis of a novel water-soluble fullerene-core starlike macromolecule via successive Michael and nucleophilic addition reaction
Piezoelectric phenomenon of fullerene–graphene bonded structure
Directly bonded hybrid of graphene nanoplatelets and fullerene: facile solid-state mechanochemical synthesis and application as carbon-based electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
First-principles study of a carbon nanobud
Effects of chemical functionalization on electronic transport in carbon nanobuds
Carbon nanobuds based on carbon nanotube caps: a first-principles study
C60 fullerenol as an active and stable catalyst for the synthesis of cyclic carbonates from CO2 and epoxides
Fullerene-grafted graphene for efficient bulk heterojunction polymer photovoltaic devices
Analysis of the reactivity and selectivity of fullerene dimerization reactions at the atomic level
In situ observations of fullerene fusion and ejection in carbon nanotubes
Synthesis, characterization, and properties of large-area graphene films
Graphene-based conducting inks for direct inkjet printing of flexible conductive patterns and their applications in electric circuits and chemical sensors
Inkjet printing of high conductivity, flexible graphene patterns
Chemical methods for the production of graphenes
Electrochemical catalysis at low dimensional carbons: Graphene, carbon nanotubes and beyond – a review
Synthesis procedure and type of graphite oxide strongly influence resulting graphene properties
Electrocatalytic activity and simultaneous determination of catechol and hydroquinone at mesoporous platinum electrode
Simultaneous determination of hydroquinone and catechol using an electrode modified by a composite of graphene/lanthanum hydroxide nanowires
Simultaneous electrochemical detection of catechol and hydroquinone based on gold nanoparticles@carbon nanocages modified electrode
Reduced graphene oxide-carbon dots composite as an enhanced material for electrochemical determination of dopamine
Biomolecules electrochemical sensing properties of a PMo11V@N-doped few layer graphene nanocomposite
Fullerene hollow microspheres prepared by bubble-templates as sensitive and selective electrocatalytic sensor for biomolecules
